title: The translator's question
summary: A literary translator and the living author whose book she is translating disagree, carefully, about one sentence — and what it reveals about the whole.

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: I have been working on chapter eleven for three weeks. I owe you a question about one sentence.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Three weeks on a sentence is either an emergency or a compliment. Which is it?

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: Both, perhaps. The sentence is the one where the father stops on the bridge. In your Norwegian, the verb 'å vente' carries two readings simultaneously — he is waiting for something, and he is simply being there, present, in the small grammatical sense of waiting that survives in your dialect. English cannot do both.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Pick one.

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: That is what I have been refusing to do for three weeks. Which one is more important to you?

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Vera, I have to be honest. When I wrote that sentence, I was not consciously balancing the two readings. The verb came to me. I wrote it down. The doubleness is real, but it is not mine. It is the language's.

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: But the doubleness is what makes the scene.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Possibly. Or the scene survives the loss of it. Translators always think one sentence is the hinge. Sometimes it is. Sometimes it is one of three or four, and removing any of them lightly damages the chapter but does not destroy it.

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: Then let me ask the question differently. If you were writing the chapter today, in English, knowing what English allows, would you place the weight on waiting-for-something, or on simply-being-there?

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: That is a better question. Simply-being-there. The father is not, in his head, waiting for anything specific. He has stopped because he cannot go on. The 'waiting' is closer to a stalled state than to an expectation.

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: Then I will translate it as standing. Not waiting.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Standing loses the time. Waiting at least implies that he is there over a stretch of minutes, not a snapshot.

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: Lingering, then.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Lingering is too soft. Lingering is what a tourist does in front of a painting.

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: Standing for a long time without moving.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Now you are writing prose I would not write.

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: Yes. I always am. That is the job. The choice is between losing the doubleness — which you say is the language's, not yours — and writing a sentence you would not have written, but that produces, in English, the effect the original produced in Norwegian.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Phrased that way, the choice is obvious. You are not translating my sentences. You are translating my reader's experience of my sentences. They are different objects, and a good translator chooses the second when the two pull in different directions.

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: That is exactly what I would like you to allow me to do — in writing, signed, in case anyone ever asks. There are reviewers who will weigh single sentences against the original, and I would rather not defend the choice alone.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: I will send a note to your publisher tomorrow. I will also send a copy to my editor, who is the person most likely to argue with you in the future.

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: Thank you. I am sorry to have been pedantic for three weeks.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: You have not been pedantic. You have been the only person who has read the chapter that closely since I wrote it.

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: Then I have a follow-up. There are eleven more sentences in this book where the same problem occurs. Some I have solved. Some I have not. Can we have this conversation eleven more times?

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Yes. But not all at once. I would like to think between them.

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: That was going to be my next condition.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Vera, may I ask something?

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: Please.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: Do you think this book is good?

body.speaker: Vera
body.text: I would not have spent three weeks on a verb in chapter eleven if I did not.

body.speaker: Lars
body.text: That is the answer I needed.

annotations.meaningVi: tính song trùng / hai tầng nghĩa cùng lúc
trạng thái đứng yên (như xe chết máy)
kỹ tính / câu nệ chi tiết một cách phiền phức
bằng văn bản, có chữ ký
khoảng thời gian kéo dài (nhiều phút)

grammarNotes.title: Hypothetical conditionals and counterfactual reframing
grammarNotes.bodyVi: C1 dùng câu điều kiện loại 2 ('If you were writing today, would you…') để mở ra một góc nhìn giả định, từ đó kéo người đối thoại ra khỏi thế bí. Trong bài: 'If you were writing the chapter today, in English, knowing what English allows, would you place the weight on waiting-for-something, or on simply-being-there?' Câu này không hỏi điều đã xảy ra — nó tạo một thực tế giả định để khai thác.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: C1 speakers use the second conditional ('If you were writing today, would you…') to construct a useful hypothetical that breaks a stuck negotiation. Vera's pivotal move: 'If you were writing the chapter today, in English, knowing what English allows, would you place the weight on waiting-for-something, or on simply-being-there?' The counterfactual frame relocates the question from 'what is the right translation?' to 'what effect would you choose?'

translationVi:
Vera: Tôi đã làm việc với chương mười một ba tuần. Tôi nợ ông một câu hỏi về một câu văn.
Lars: Ba tuần cho một câu thì hoặc là khủng hoảng hoặc là lời khen. Cái nào vậy?
Vera: Có lẽ cả hai. Câu đó là câu người cha dừng lại trên cây cầu. Trong tiếng Na Uy của ông, động từ 'å vente' mang đồng thời hai nghĩa — ông ấy đang chờ một điều gì đó, và ông ấy đơn giản là đang ở đó, có mặt, theo cái nghĩa ngữ pháp nhỏ của 'chờ' vẫn còn sống trong phương ngữ của ông. Tiếng Anh không thể làm cả hai.
Lars: Chọn một thôi.
Vera: Đó là điều tôi đã từ chối làm trong ba tuần. Cái nào quan trọng với ông hơn?
Lars: Vera, tôi phải thành thật. Khi tôi viết câu đó, tôi đã không cân nhắc một cách ý thức hai nghĩa đó. Động từ đến với tôi. Tôi viết xuống. Tính song trùng có thật, nhưng nó không phải của tôi. Nó là của ngôn ngữ.
Vera: Nhưng tính song trùng là cái làm nên cảnh đó.
Lars: Có thể. Hoặc cảnh sống sót dù mất nó. Người dịch luôn nghĩ một câu là bản lề. Đôi khi là vậy. Đôi khi nó là một trong ba bốn câu, và bỏ bất cứ câu nào trong số đó sẽ làm chương bị thương nhẹ chứ không huỷ hoại nó.
Vera: Vậy để tôi hỏi câu hỏi khác đi. Nếu hôm nay ông viết chương này, bằng tiếng Anh, biết những gì tiếng Anh cho phép, ông sẽ đặt sức nặng vào 'chờ-một-điều-gì-đó', hay vào 'đơn-giản-có-mặt-ở-đó'?
Lars: Đó là một câu hỏi tốt hơn. 'Đơn-giản-có-mặt-ở-đó'. Người cha, trong đầu, không chờ điều gì cụ thể. Ông ấy đã dừng lại vì ông không thể đi tiếp. 'Chờ' ở đây gần với trạng thái đứng-khựng hơn là chờ-mong.
Vera: Vậy tôi sẽ dịch là 'standing' — đứng. Không phải 'waiting'.
Lars: 'Standing' mất thời gian. 'Waiting' ít nhất ngụ ý ông ấy ở đó trong một khoảng vài phút, không phải một khoảnh khắc.
Vera: Vậy 'lingering' — nán lại.
Lars: 'Lingering' quá mềm. 'Lingering' là điều một khách du lịch làm trước một bức tranh.
Vera: 'Standing for a long time without moving' — đứng lâu mà không nhúc nhích.
Lars: Bây giờ cô đang viết thứ văn xuôi mà tôi sẽ không viết.
Vera: Vâng. Tôi luôn vậy. Đó là việc của tôi. Lựa chọn là giữa mất đi tính song trùng — mà ông nói là của ngôn ngữ, không phải của ông — và viết một câu ông sẽ không viết, nhưng tạo ra, trong tiếng Anh, hiệu quả mà nguyên tác tạo ra trong tiếng Na Uy.
Lars: Diễn đạt như vậy, lựa chọn rõ ràng. Cô không dịch câu của tôi. Cô dịch trải nghiệm của người đọc với câu của tôi. Đó là hai vật khác nhau, và một dịch giả tốt chọn cái thứ hai khi hai cái kéo về hai hướng khác nhau.
Vera: Đó chính xác là điều tôi muốn ông cho phép tôi làm — bằng văn bản, có chữ ký, phòng khi có ai hỏi. Có những nhà phê bình sẽ cân từng câu với nguyên tác, và tôi không muốn một mình bảo vệ lựa chọn ấy.
Lars: Mai tôi sẽ gửi thư cho nhà xuất bản của cô. Tôi cũng sẽ gửi một bản cho biên tập viên của tôi, người dễ tranh luận với cô nhất trong tương lai.
Vera: Cảm ơn. Tôi xin lỗi vì đã câu nệ suốt ba tuần.
Lars: Cô không câu nệ. Cô là người duy nhất đã đọc chương đó cẩn thận như vậy kể từ khi tôi viết nó.
Vera: Vậy tôi có câu tiếp. Còn mười một câu nữa trong cuốn sách này có cùng vấn đề. Một số tôi đã giải. Một số tôi chưa. Chúng ta có thể có cuộc trò chuyện này thêm mười một lần nữa được không?
Lars: Được. Nhưng không phải tất cả cùng lúc. Tôi muốn được suy nghĩ giữa các lần.
Vera: Đó là điều kiện kế tiếp tôi định nêu.
Lars: Vera, tôi hỏi một điều được không?
Vera: Ông cứ hỏi.
Lars: Cô có nghĩ cuốn sách này tốt không?
Vera: Tôi đã không bỏ ba tuần cho một động từ ở chương mười một nếu tôi không nghĩ vậy.
Lars: Đó là câu trả lời tôi cần.

questions.prompt: How long has Vera spent on the single sentence?
questions.explanation: 'I have been working on chapter eleven for three weeks.'

questions.prompt: What is the technical problem with the Norwegian verb 'å vente'?
options: It carries two readings at once — waiting for something, and simply being present — and English cannot do both.
It does not exist in Lars's dialect.
It is too long to translate.
questions.explanation: 'In your Norwegian, the verb 'å vente' carries two readings simultaneously… English cannot do both.'

questions.prompt: How does Lars characterise his own creation of the doubleness?
options: It is real but not his — it belongs to the language; he was not consciously balancing two readings.
He stole the device from another writer.
He does not believe it exists.
questions.explanation: 'The doubleness is real, but it is not mine. It is the language's.'
questions.hint: Lars's longer turn after Vera asks which reading is more important.

questions.prompt: What general claim does Lars make about translators?
options: They always think one sentence is the hinge; sometimes it is, sometimes it is one of several whose loss damages but does not destroy a chapter.
They never read the chapter twice.
They focus on the wrong languages.
questions.explanation: 'Translators always think one sentence is the hinge. Sometimes it is. Sometimes it is one of three or four…'
questions.hint: Lars's reply about the scene 'surviving' the loss.

questions.prompt: How does Vera reframe the question to break the impasse?
options: If Lars were writing today in English, knowing what English allows, would he place the weight on waiting-for-something or on simply-being-there?
She asks him to choose another verb entirely.
She asks him to abandon the sentence.
She asks him for permission to skip the sentence.
questions.explanation: Direct quote of Vera's second conditional reframing.
questions.hint: Look for Vera's 'If you were writing today…' question.

questions.prompt: Which reading does Lars finally choose?
options: Simply-being-there — the father has stopped because he cannot go on.
Waiting-for-something — the father is expecting a visitor.
Neither — he wants Vera to drop the sentence.
questions.explanation: 'Simply-being-there. The father is not, in his head, waiting for anything specific.'
questions.hint: Lars's reply to the reframed question.

questions.prompt: Why does Lars reject 'standing' as the English translation?
options: 'Standing' loses the sense of time; 'waiting' at least implies a stretch of minutes, not a snapshot.
questions.explanation: 'Standing loses the time. Waiting at least implies that he is there over a stretch of minutes, not a snapshot.'

options: 'Lingering is too soft. Lingering is what a tourist does in front of a painting.'

questions.prompt: What does Lars mean by 'You are not translating my sentences. You are translating my reader's experience of my sentences. They are different objects'?
options: The legitimate target of translation is the effect on a reader, not literal sentence-equivalence.
Vera is translating someone else's book.
Sentences cannot be translated at all.
questions.explanation: He explicitly separates the textual object from the experiential object.
questions.hint: Look at how he distinguishes 'my sentences' from 'my reader's experience.'

questions.prompt: What does Vera ask Lars to do, formally?
options: Send a note to her publisher giving her permission, in writing and signed.
questions.explanation: 'In writing, signed, in case anyone ever asks.'

questions.prompt: How does Lars respond to Vera's apology for being 'pedantic for three weeks'?
options: He says she has not been pedantic — she has been the only person who has read the chapter that closely since he wrote it.
He agrees that she has wasted time.
He says she should have read other chapters.
questions.explanation: 'You have not been pedantic. You have been the only person who has read the chapter that closely since I wrote it.'
questions.hint: Lars's reply right after the apology.

questions.prompt: How many more such sentences does Vera report in the book?
questions.explanation: 'There are eleven more sentences in this book where the same problem occurs.'

questions.prompt: What is Lars's condition for having the conversation eleven more times?
options: Not all at once — he wants time to think between them.
He wants Vera to come to Norway.
He wants to write the translations himself.
questions.explanation: 'Yes. But not all at once. I would like to think between them.'
questions.hint: Lars's response to the request for more conversations.

questions.prompt: How does Vera answer Lars's final question, 'Do you think this book is good?'
options: 'I would not have spent three weeks on a verb in chapter eleven if I did not.'
questions.explanation: Direct quote of Vera's final reply.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the dialogue NOT claiming?
options: That translators should always pick the literal equivalent of a foreign word.
That the value of a translated sentence lies in the reader's experience.
That deep readers can be rarer than authors — Vera reads more closely than anyone since Lars wrote the chapter.
That counterfactual questions can unstick stuck negotiations.
questions.explanation: The dialogue argues the opposite: not literal equivalence, but equivalence of reader experience.
questions.hint: Look at the exchange about 'my sentences' vs 'my reader's experience.'

cloze.template:
Vera: I have been working on chapter eleven for three {{b1}}. I owe you a question about one sentence.
Lars: Three weeks on a sentence is either an emergency or a {{b2}}. Which is it?
Vera: Both, perhaps. The sentence is the one where the father stops on the bridge. In your Norwegian, the verb 'å vente' carries two readings {{b3}} — he is waiting for something, and he is simply being there, present, in the small grammatical sense of waiting that survives in your dialect. English cannot do both.
Lars: Pick one.
Vera: That is what I have been refusing to do for three weeks. Which one is more important to you?
Lars: Vera, I have to be honest. When I wrote that sentence, I was not consciously {{b4}} the two readings. The verb came to me. I wrote it down. The doubleness is real, but it is not mine. It is the {{b5}}'s.
Vera: But the doubleness is what makes the scene.
Lars: Possibly. Or the scene survives the loss of it. Translators always think one sentence is the {{b6}}. Sometimes it is. Sometimes it is one of three or four, and removing any of them lightly damages the chapter but does not destroy it.
Vera: Then let me ask the question differently. If you were writing the chapter today, in English, knowing what English allows, would you place the weight on waiting-for-something, or on simply-being-{{b7}}?
Lars: That is a better question. Simply-being-there. The father is not, in his head, waiting for anything specific. He has stopped because he cannot go on. The 'waiting' is closer to a stalled {{b8}} than to an expectation.
Vera: Then I will translate it as standing. Not waiting.
Lars: Standing loses the {{b9}}. Waiting at least implies that he is there over a stretch of minutes, not a snapshot.
Vera: Lingering, then.
Lars: Lingering is too {{b10}}. Lingering is what a tourist does in front of a painting.
Vera: Standing for a long time without moving.
Lars: Now you are writing prose I would not write.
Vera: Yes. I always am. That is the job. The choice is between losing the doubleness — which you say is the language's, not yours — and writing a sentence you would not have written, but that produces, in English, the {{b11}} the original produced in Norwegian.
Lars: Phrased that way, the choice is obvious. You are not translating my sentences. You are translating my reader's {{b12}} of my sentences. They are different objects, and a good translator chooses the second when the two pull in different directions.
Vera: That is exactly what I would like you to allow me to do — in writing, {{b13}}, in case anyone ever asks. There are reviewers who will weigh single sentences against the original, and I would rather not defend the choice alone.
Lars: I will send a note to your publisher tomorrow. I will also send a copy to my editor, who is the person most likely to argue with you in the future.
Vera: Thank you. I am sorry to have been pedantic for three weeks.
Lars: You have not been pedantic. You have been the only person who has read the chapter that closely since I wrote it.
Vera: Then I have a follow-up. There are {{b14}} more sentences in this book where the same problem occurs. Some I have solved. Some I have not. Can we have this conversation eleven more times?
Lars: Yes. But not all at once. I would like to {{b15}} between them.
Vera: That was going to be my next condition.
Lars: Vera, may I ask something?
Vera: Please.
Lars: Do you think this book is good?
Vera: I would not have spent three weeks on a {{b16}} in chapter eleven if I did not.
Lars: That is the answer I needed.

blanks.explanation: Either an emergency or a compliment.
He wasn't consciously balancing the two readings.
Translators think one sentence is the hinge.
A stalled state, not an expectation.
The reader's experience of his sentences.
He would like to think between conversations.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Lars says 'You are not translating my sentences. You are translating my reader's experience of my sentences.' Is there an act in your own life — explaining a parent's silence to a sibling, recounting a meeting to your boss — where the right unit of fidelity is the listener's experience rather than the literal words? What freedoms does that license, and what dishonesty would it tolerate that ought not to be tolerated?
